original_abstract:
This review examines *Private and Domestic Devotion in Early Modern Britain*, a collection of essays edited by Jessica Martin and Alec Ryrie that shifts the scholarly focus from institutional and doctrinal history toward the lived religious experiences of individuals and families. While much of the recent literature on the British Reformation has prioritized structural shifts in theology and ecclesiology, this volume explores how Reformed faith was articulated within the household through solitary prayer, family worship, and the use of devotional objects. The contributors demonstrate that the domestic sphere complicates traditional binary distinctions—such as Catholic versus Protestant or elite versus popular—by revealing a landscape of piety where institutional mandates often merged with personal innovation and vestiges of pre-Reformation tradition. 

The essays span a wide range of topics, including the persistent influence of the penitential psalms, the role of visual aids in Reformed homes, and the cultural anxieties surrounding the secrecy of private prayer. From the regulation of reading practices to evangelical interpretations of sleep and dreams, the collection illustrates how the Reformation reshaped the interior lives of believers in England and Scotland. Despite a primary focus on the seventeenth century and a heavy emphasis on evangelical rather than Catholic or Welsh contexts, the reviewer commends the volume for addressing a significant gap in Reformation studies. Ultimately, the collection argues that a comprehensive understanding of early modern religious change is impossible without accounting for the diverse and often hybrid practices of domestic devotion.
humanized_abstract:
This review is of Private and Domestic Devotion in Early Modern Britain, a collection of essays edited by Jessica Martin and Alec Ryrie. Building on the scholarly shift away from institutional and doctrinal history in order to gain a more detailed understanding of individual and domestic religious lives, this book of essays explores how religion was expressed in the private and domestic spaces of Reformed Britain. Most recent accounts of the British Reformation have tended to concentrate on changes to doctrine and ecclesiology; here, the domestic and its associated practices of private prayer, domestic worship and devotion to devotional objects are foregrounded. The result is a rich tapestry of representations of domestic devotional practices which powerfully complicate the dichotomies of Catholic and Protestant or even elite and popular devotional styles. Instead, the domestic provided a space in which religious practices may have drawn upon official instructions and decrees, but also offered opportunity for devotional creativity. At the same time, domestic devotional practices also acted as a residual register of pre-Reformation practices and convictions, which stretched back into the centuries before the Reformation, further complicating our understanding of the manner in which devotion evolved over time in early modern Britain.

Studies in the Cultures of Early Modern Literatures Edited by Mario Di Gangi, Thomas Sherman Cover image Other titles available The essays here offer a fascinating array of readings into the cultural dynamics of Protestant England and early modern Scotland from the latter part of the sixteenth through into the eighteenth century, engaging a whole range of concerns from the enduring influence of the penitential psalms in contemporary devotion, the use of images in a Reformed domestic context, the fears and unease attendant on the private worship of God in secret and beyond the purview of ecclesiastical discipline or church audit, reading practices and the ways they came to be policed; through to a host of peculiar and little known evangelical concerns, including interpretations of sleep and dreams. Predominantly concerned with the17th century, and overwhelmingly focussed upon evangelical developments and concerns in Protestant England and early modern Scotland to the relative exclusion of the Catholic and Welsh devotional traditions, this intriguing collection of studies more than redeemed the space which has existed for some years now in the literature on the Reformation as a series of transformations to individual religious experience in early modern Britain. Through a range of methodologies and examples the volumes various chapters demonstrate by a variety of means the domestic space of devotion was frequently a hybridised and hybridizing zone, the absence of which from analyses of early modern religious change reduces our comprehension of that religion profoundly.
